one_line_takeaway: ShellForge shows that iteratively co-training a trainable PHP-webshell generator against a three-view (semantic/AST/statistical) fusion detector, combined with LLM-generated 'de-malicious' hard negatives, both raises adversarial robustness (0.9818 accuracy on hard EvoSet vs ~0.55 for statically trained baselines) and cuts false positives on obfuscated-but-benign code from 86.24% to 2.02% FPR.
key_finding: On the static FWOID test set, ShellForge's detector reaches 0.9936 accuracy / 0.9940 precision / 0.9920 recall / 0.9930 F1, beating GlareShell (0.9860 F1), AST-DF (0.9830 F1), FRF-WD (0.9819 F1), and ShellPub (0.8874 F1). On EvoSet (adversarially harder, distribution-shifted samples), models trained only on static FWOID without co-training drop to roughly 0.55 accuracy (per Table 2's caption); after adversarial co-training all trainable baselines improve substantially (e.g., GlareShell 0.9476 accuracy / 0.9433 F1, +0.4063/+0.2817 gain), but ShellForge attains the best post-co-training robustness at 0.9818 accuracy (+0.4297) and 0.9805 F1 (+0.2996). Over 6 co-evolution rounds, detector F1 rises from 0.681 to 0.981 while the number of successful generator evasions per round declines and the surviving evasive samples grow longer/more complex (per Figure 6); the converged generator achieves a 0.939 evasion rate against VirusTotal commercial engines. Ablations: (a) de-malicious negatives trade a small amount of in-distribution F1 (0.9930 -> 0.9805 on FWOID) for a large false-positive reduction on a held-out de-malicious benign set (86.24% FPR -> 2.02% FPR); (b) among long-text handling strategies, head-only truncation and compression-only/head+tail-only each underperform, while compression+head-tail together reaches 0.9879 F1 and the full pipeline (with the CodeBERT/AST/stats fusion) reaches 0.9930 F1; (c) among single views, AST-only gets 0.8693 F1 (high recall, low precision), stats-only only 0.6536 F1, source-only 0.9709 F1, and full three-view fusion is needed to reach 0.9930 F1 (two-view combos top out at 0.9819 F1 for Source+Stats).
core_contribution: ShellForge is an adversarial co-evolution framework for PHP webshell defense that couples (1) a trainable webshell generator (Qwen2.5-Coder, SFT then PPO-style preference optimization with webshell-as-chosen / benign-as-rejected signals) with (2) a multi-view fusion detector combining a CodeBERT+TextCNN semantic branch over compressed/head-tail-truncated source, a Tree-sitter AST-sequence branch (pruned, truncated arrays, string-node children dropped) encoded with a second TextCNN, and a 14-dim global statistical branch (risky-function counts, line-length stats, Shannon entropy) fused by concatenation and an MLP classifier. The two components are updated iteratively: at each round the detector scores generator candidates; samples it already catches (score >= threshold delta) become rejected preference signals for the generator and hard positives for the detector, while samples that evade (score < delta) become chosen signals for the generator and, after an LLM-based 'de-malicious transformation' T(.) that strips harmful sinks (command exec, file write, eval) while preserving surrounding obfuscation, are added as hard negatives (benign-but-obfuscated) for the detector. The de-malicious transformation is implemented with prompt-based rewriting by Claude Sonnet 4.5 followed by an independent verification pass using Gemini 3.0 to filter samples that still exhibit harmful behavior. The stated purpose is to reduce false positives caused by detectors treating obfuscation itself as a maliciousness signal ('obfuscation bias').
threat_model: Defender operates a static/behavioral PHP webshell classifier deployed against attackers who continually mutate webshell surface form (multi-layer encoding, long string literals, inert padding, structure-preserving obfuscation) to evade signature- and ML-based detection while preserving functional maliciousness. A secondary concern is production false positives: benign administrative/commercial PHP code that is heavily obfuscated for IP protection or license enforcement resembles malicious obfuscation patterns and must not be flagged. The paper explicitly frames the generator as a red-teaming/training component subordinate to hardening the detector, not as a standalone offensive contribution ('the generator primarily serves the new co-training framework rather than being a standalone offensive capability').
limitations: Single-author paper; evaluated on one benchmark (FWOID) derived from prior work by the same overlapping author group, so external validity to other webshell corpora or non-PHP shells is untested (the paper itself notes existing surveys highlight a bias toward PHP and a lack of standardized cross-validation benchmarks in the field generally). The de-malicious transformation and its verification pass depend on two proprietary third-party LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.5 for rewriting, Gemini 3.0 for verification) whose own error/leakage rates on this task are not separately quantified beyond the downstream FPR numbers. The VirusTotal evasion-rate metric characterizes the generator against a snapshot of commercial engines at test time, which is a moving target and not reproducible over time. Training/compute setup is modest (single consumer GPU, 10 epochs) and hyperparameters such as the exchange threshold delta and the number of co-training rounds (six) are not subjected to sensitivity analysis in the excerpted text. The paper does not report statistical significance/variance across seeds (all experiments use 'the same random seed'), so point-estimate gains (e.g., +0.4297 accuracy on EvoSet) lack confidence intervals.

datasets_used: FWOID (5,001 webshell + 5,936 benign PHP samples, from prior work ref [39]), stratified 80/10/10 split
EvoSet: co-training-accumulated hard-sample set, 1,138 evasive webshells + 1,090 de-malicious benign samples at convergence
Combined FWOID+EvoSet pool: 6,139 webshell + 7,026 benign samples
Held-out de-malicious benign test set: 1,090 samples, obfuscated but non-malicious
quantitative_results: FWOID test set (detector): Accuracy 0.9936, Precision 0.9940, Recall 0.9920, F1 0.9930
EvoSet (post co-training, detector): Accuracy 0.9818 (+0.4297 vs static-only), Precision 0.9773 (+0.4421), Recall 0.9837 (+0.0478), F1 0.9805 (+0.2996)
Generator overall evasion rate against VirusTotal commercial engines: 0.939
Detector F1 across 6 co-evolution rounds: rises from 0.681 to 0.981
De-malicious ablation: without de-malicious negatives, FWOID F1 0.9930 but FPR on de-malicious benign set 86.24%; with strategy, FWOID F1 0.9805 and FPR 2.02%
Long-text ablation: Head only 0.9690 F1; Compress only 0.9699 F1; Head+Tail 0.9699 F1; Head+Compress 0.9879 F1; full ShellForge 0.9930 F1
View-fusion ablation: Source only 0.9709 F1; AST only 0.8693 F1; Stats only 0.6536 F1; Source+AST 0.9768 F1; Source+Stats 0.9819 F1; full three-view fusion 0.9930 F1
baselines_compared: ShellPub (production rule-matching + static inspection engine): FWOID F1 0.8874; EvoSet F1 0.8083 (static engine, not retrained)
AST-DF (AST n-gram/TF-IDF + Deep Forest): FWOID F1 0.9830; EvoSet F1 0.9260 (+0.3295 after co-training)
FRF-WD (PHP opcode + FastText + random forest): FWOID F1 0.9819; EvoSet F1 0.8990 (+0.2483 after co-training)
GlareShell (interprocedural CFG + attention GNN): FWOID F1 0.9860; EvoSet F1 0.9433 (+0.2817 after co-training)
